name: Ukemochi
description: Ukemochi-no-Kami, often shortened to Ukemochi, is the Shinto goddess of food. She is a vital figure in Japanese mythology, responsible for providing sustenance and nourishment to the people. Her origins are not explicitly detailed in the earliest texts, but she appears in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the two primary sources of Japanese mythology. Ukemochi is associated with abundance and fertility. Her death, though violent, led to the creation of essential food sources: rice, wheat, beans, millet, azuki beans, soybeans, and silkworms sprung from her body. The goddess's story highlights the cyclical nature of life and death, and how sacrifice can lead to prosperity. She is often venerated in agricultural communities, where rituals are performed to ensure a bountiful harvest. The story of Ukemochi also carries a cautionary tale about the proper way to offer food, emphasizing respect and gratitude for the gifts of nature. Her power lies in her ability to generate food, making her an essential deity in a society deeply connected to agriculture. She is considered a benevolent figure, albeit one whose demise was necessary for the well-being of humanity. In some interpretations, she is seen as a manifestation of the earth's inherent capacity to provide for its inhabitants. The tale of Ukemochi serves as a reminder of the importance of respecting nature and appreciating the gifts it provides.
subtitle: Goddess of Food and Sustenance

keyMyths.title: Ukemochi's Death and the Origin of Food
keyMyths.description: Ukemochi was killed by Tsukuyomi after he was disgusted by the way she produced food. From her corpse sprang the first grains and livestock, essential for human survival.

keyMyths.title: Tsukuyomi's Disgust
keyMyths.description: Tsukuyomi visited Ukemochi and was horrified to see her produce food from her mouth, nose, and anus. This act of perceived impropriety led him to slay her.

keyMyths.title: The Connection to Inari
keyMyths.description: Ukemochi is sometimes associated with Inari, the god of rice and agriculture. This connection reinforces her role as a provider of sustenance and a protector of crops.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, an important source for Japanese mythology.
